items.question: Who needs categorical encoding in a machine learning pipeline?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers preparing tabular, survey, or log features for algorithms that expect numeric inputs—especially linear models, neural nets on mixed features, and classic sklearn tree implementations.

items.question: What is categorical encoding?
items.answer: The step that maps discrete labels (strings or codes) into numeric representations—one-hot columns for nominal variables, ordered integers for ordinal variables, or cross-validated target statistics for high-cardinality fields.

items.question: When should you use ordinal instead of one-hot encoding?
items.answer: When category levels have a defensible order (e.g. low < medium < high) and you want the model to treat equal steps similarly; never when levels are purely nominal names with no ranking.

items.question: Where in the pipeline should encoders be fit?
items.answer: On the training split only (or inner train folds for target encoding); the same learned vocabulary and mappings transform validation, test, and production rows without peeking at holdout labels or distributions.

items.question: Why is naive target encoding dangerous?
items.answer: Replacing a category with the mean target of all rows in that category—including the current row—puts label information directly into features, leaking the target and inflating offline scores while failing in production.

items.question: How does one-hot encoding represent a nominal column?
items.answer: Each category level gets a binary column; for a given row exactly one of those columns is 1 (others 0), so the model learns separate effects per level without assuming one level is 'greater' than another.

items.question: How does one-hot encoding compare to ordinal encoding?
items.answer: One-hot creates separate binary columns per nominal level and avoids implied order; ordinal maps ordered levels to integers 0..K−1 and is compact but wrong for unordered names. Use one-hot for city or color; ordinal for size grades or Likert scales.

items.question: How does one-hot encoding compare to target encoding for high-cardinality features?
items.answer: One-hot explodes dimension (10k zip codes → 10k sparse columns) but is leakage-safe and interpretable; target encoding yields one numeric column but needs out-of-fold means and smoothing to avoid memorizing rare levels and leaking labels.
